questions.prompt: A startup launches 'your AI executive assistant' that books meetings, responds to emails, and reschedules autonomously on day one. Within a week, three users have high-profile mistakes (wrong cancellations). What went wrong?

choices.text: They picked the wrong LLM provider.

They launched at level 5 (background coworker acting autonomously) before building user trust or eval coverage. Most successful 'autonomous' products start lower (level 3 — propose then approve, or level 4 — scheduled with surfacing) and ramp up as trust and evals catch up. Level 5 in high-impact domains is the hardest place to start.

questions.explanation: Autonomy and trust are coupled. Launching at the highest autonomy level with zero trust account means the first big mistake torches the product. Successful ramp: start at level 2-3 with approval, build trust through transparency and reversibility, opt users up to higher autonomy on narrow domains where evals are strong.

questions.prompt: What's the right default autonomy level for a new assistant feature?

choices.text: Level 3 — initiative assistant: bot proposes actions, executes after approval. Lets the bot show value through proactive suggestions without taking irreversible actions. Easiest path to building user trust; easy to step up to level 4-5 on opt-in once evals and trust support it.
Skip levels and launch where competitors are.

questions.explanation: Level 3 is the sweet spot for shipping a new assistant: enough autonomy to be useful (proactive proposals) without enough to break trust (no unilateral irreversible actions). Once users like the suggestions and evals look solid, you can offer level 4 (scheduled) and level 5 (background) as opt-in upgrades on specific tasks.
